name: Medical Devices

asset_categories.description: AI/ML model making or supporting clinical decisions — diagnosis, prognosis, treatment recommendation, triage
Protected health information (PHI) data flows including FHIR/HL7/DICOM interfaces, EHR integrations
Reference datasets used for model calibration, validation, and performance monitoring in clinical context
Data, models, and documentation submitted to regulatory bodies (notified bodies, FDA) as part of device approval
Integration points with hospital IT systems — FHIR APIs, HL7v2 feeds, DICOM PACS, clinical workflows
Post-market clinical follow-up data, adverse event records, performance monitoring datasets

threat_sources.guidance: Use regulation IDs: 'MDR' for Medical Device Regulation 2017/745, 'IVDR' for In Vitro Diagnostic Regulation 2017/746, 'GDPR' for data protection. Search MDR for Annex I General Safety and Performance Requirements (GSPRs). Focus on GSPR 14 (software as medical device), GSPR 17.1-17.4 (IT security measures for devices with software). Search IVDR if the device is an in-vitro diagnostic. Search GDPR Article 9 for health data special category provisions.

impact_calibration.safety: Use IEC 62304 software safety class to calibrate: Class C (could contribute to death or serious injury) = safety 4-5. Class B (could contribute to non-serious injury) = safety 3. Class A (no contribution to hazardous situation) = safety 1-2. For SaMD: use IMDRF risk categorization (Category IV = safety 5, III = 4, II = 3, I = 2). Any model making autonomous clinical decisions without human review is minimum safety 4.
impact_calibration.financial: Include regulatory penalties (MDR: up to EUR 2M per incident per Member State), product recall costs, clinical liability, malpractice litigation exposure. Misdiagnosis at scale = financial 5.
impact_calibration.privacy: Patient health data is GDPR special category (Article 9). Any exposure of PHI or re-identification risk = privacy 4-5. Consider HIPAA if US market. Inference attacks on medical training data can reveal patient conditions.
impact_calibration.regulatory: MDR Annex I GSPRs 14.1-14.5 (software requirements) and 17.1-17.4 (cybersecurity). IEC 62304 software lifecycle. IEC 81001-5-1 security activities. FDA premarket cybersecurity guidance (2023). Non-compliance blocks CE marking or FDA clearance — cannot sell the device.
impact_calibration.ethical: Clinical AI bias can lead to disparate care outcomes across demographics. Algorithmic discrimination in diagnosis disproportionately affects underserved populations. Patient autonomy requires transparency about AI involvement in clinical decisions.

feasibility_calibration: Medical devices in hospital networks often have constrained attack surface — dedicated VLANs, network segmentation, physical access controls. Adjust window_of_opportunity upward (harder access). However, home-use and remote patient monitoring devices have broad exposure — treat as consumer-grade attack surface. SOUP (Software of Unknown Provenance) components are a significant supply chain risk — known CVEs in medical device libraries are common. DICOM and HL7v2 protocols have limited built-in security — interoperability interfaces rate higher feasibility than isolated devices.

compliance_frameworks.name: MDR 2017/745 Annex I
key_requirements: GSPR 14.2(d) — Risks from reasonably foreseeable misuse
GSPR 14.5 — Devices incorporating software or that are software
GSPR 17.1 — IT security measures including protection against unauthorized access
GSPR 17.2 — Minimum requirements for IT security (hardware, network, software)
GSPR 17.3 — Verification and validation of IT security
GSPR 17.4 — Security of the supply chain

report_addenda.title: SaMD Risk Classification
report_addenda.guidance: Determine IMDRF SaMD risk category (I-IV) based on significance of information provided (treat, diagnose, drive clinical management, inform clinical management) and healthcare situation (critical, serious, non-serious). Map to IEC 62304 software safety class (A/B/C). This classification drives the depth of security activities required by IEC 81001-5-1.

report_addenda.title: MDR GSPR Cybersecurity Traceability
report_addenda.guidance: Map each R3-R5 threat to the MDR Annex I GSPRs it could violate (primarily 14.1-14.5 and 17.1-17.4). For each GSPR, show: which threats are relevant, what controls address them, and whether the GSPR is satisfied, partially satisfied, or not addressed. This traceability is required by notified bodies during conformity assessment.

report_addenda.title: IEC 81001-5-1 Security Activity Mapping
report_addenda.guidance: Map TARA phases to IEC 81001-5-1 security activities: Phase 1 → 5.2 (risk management process), Phase 2 → 5.2 (threat identification), Phase 3-5 → 5.2 (risk evaluation), Phase 6 → 5.3 (security requirements) + 6.2 (secure design), Phase 7 → 9.2 (security guidance documentation). Show which security activities are covered by the TARA output.
